title: Body scan for sleep
subtitle: A ten-minute meditation that walks attention from feet to crown.
excerpt: A slow guided body scan for the evening, drawn from secular mindfulness traditions. Used as a wind-down rather than a sleep technique: the practice settles the body and gives the mind somewhere to rest while it does.
sourceNotes: Body scan meditation has multiple lineages. The version here draws on the secular mindfulness adaptations that became widespread through Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) in the late 1970s and onwards. The framing as a sleep wind-down rather than a daytime mindfulness sit takes its lead from Day 3 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use in the last ten minutes before sleep, when the body is still wired but the day has been put down.
practice.whenNotToUse: If you tend to dissociate from your body during practices like this, use a feet-on-floor grounding instead. The body scan asks attention to move through every part of the body, which can be too much in a hard week.

glossaryTerms.definition: A meditation practice in which attention is moved slowly from one part of the body to the next, noticing sensation without trying to change it. Long established in contemplative traditions; popularised in secular form through MBSR in the late 1970s.

content.text: The body scan is a slow walk of attention from one part of the body to the next. It isn't a sleep technique exactly. It is something to give the mind to do while the body lets go of the day. Used regularly before sleep, most people fall asleep before the scan finishes. That is fine. The practice has done its work either way.
Read the practice aloud once before you try it, so the order is in your head. After a few nights you will know it by heart and can do it without reading.
The practice
Setting up
Lie down in bed, on your back if your body allows it. Pillow under the knees if your lower back is tender. Arms by your sides, palms up if that feels easy. Close your eyes or let them soften.
Take three slow breaths. Let the first one settle the body into the mattress. Let the second one drop the shoulders. Let the third one soften the jaw.
The scan
Bring your attention to the soles of your feet. Not to change anything, only to notice. Warmth or coolness. Pressure where they meet the bed. The skin against the sheet.
Move slowly up the body, staying with each part for a few breaths before moving on:
the tops of your feet, the ankles
the calves, behind the knees
the thighs, where they meet the bed
the hips, the lower back
the belly, rising and falling
the chest, rising and falling
the fingertips, then up through the hands and wrists
the forearms, the elbows, the upper arms
the shoulders, where they meet the bed
the neck, the back of the head
the jaw, the cheeks, the small muscles around the eyes
the forehead, the top of the head
When the attention wanders off (which it will), notice that it has wandered. Bring it back to the part of the body you were on. The wandering isn't a problem. The noticing is the practice.
Coming back

If you have made it to the top of the head and you are still awake, rest the attention on the whole body for a few breaths. Feel the bed under you. Feel the air at the edge of your skin. Let the next breath be a longer exhale than inhale. Then the next one. Then the next one.
If you are still awake after that, the scan has done its work even if sleep hasn't arrived. Some nights are like that. Lying with a softened body and a quiet mind is rest, even when it isn't sleep.
What you might notice

Most nights you will fall asleep somewhere around the belly or the chest. Other nights you'll get all the way to the top of the head and stay awake; that is normal. A few nights you'll find a part of the body that feels gripped (often the jaw or the right shoulder); spend an extra breath or two there before moving on.
Don't grade the practice. There isn't a good scan and a bad scan. There is the scan you did tonight.
Where this practice comes from

Body scan meditation appears across multiple contemplative lineages. The version most familiar in the West passed through Jon Kabat-Zinn's Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction programme in the late 1970s, which adapted earlier Buddhist insight-meditation forms into a secular clinical practice. From there it has spread widely through hospital and workplace settings. The version here is the secular bedtime adaptation, used as a sleep wind-down rather than a daytime mindfulness sit. The framing as a sleep practice rather than an alertness practice borrows its emphasis from Day 3 of Rebecca's
SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive
